title: Renting the overbuild: does an IG balance sheet make a better neocloud?
question: When a cash-rich hyperscaler like Meta starts renting out its spare AI compute, does its cheaper money and its own built-in demand quietly beat the pure-play neocloud (CoreWeave, Nebius) — and if the durable rents are sliding down the stack, what does that mean for the rest of the sector and for US-vs-China AI?
finding: An investment-grade balance sheet makes a structurally better neocloud, and the pure-play is a spread business wearing a growth costume. On comparable senior unsecured debt Meta funds at 5.625% (its 30-year bond) and even private SpaceX at 5.35-6.65% (Baa1/BBB+/BBB, on a $25bn debut IG bond) while pure-play CoreWeave (B+) pays 9.00-9.75% - a ~300-425bp gap on the identical GPUs, wider against its older secured loans. The honest caveat that keeps it conditional: CoreWeave engineered its newest $8.5bn GPU-backed facility to an IG rating (A3/A-low) near 5.9%, so the gap narrows at the best-structured edge, and Oracle shows even IG is no shelter (5yr CDS to a record ~198bp on -$23.7bn free cash flow). Three premise corrections sharpen it rather than weaken it: 'Meta is selling compute' is optionality not product (today Meta is a big neocloud BUYER - CoreWeave ~$21bn, Nebius ~$27bn, Crusoe ~1.6GW); Meta did not quit models, it quit OPEN-WEIGHT leadership (shipped the closed Muse Spark, hedged internally with rivals' models, and was cut off from Google's Gemini); and the captive-build-then-rent first mover was xAI/SpaceX, not 'Grok owned by Tesla' (Colossus leased to Anthropic $1.25bn/mo + Google $920m/mo, ~$2.17bn/mo, IG-funded, before Meta's plan surfaced). The market agreed on July 1, 2026: on the Meta Compute report Meta rose ~9-10% while CoreWeave fell ~13% and Nebius ~15% (reported, not yet market-adjusted; both neoclouds were UP the day before). The forecast: (1) bearish on the pure-play neocloud equity story - squeezed between hyperscaler cost-of-capital above and a commoditizing rental price below (McKinsey/Contrary flag ~20% not surviving 2026-27), with ~23-24% short interest and its own biggest customers turning into competitors - but conditional, not an outright short (contracted backlog, real scarcity, narrowing funding cost); (2) the rents migrate DOWN to power/land, chip IP, and IG captive-demand compute, away from the model labs and the levered single-customer neoclouds; (3) on US vs China it is repositioning, not surrender - the West ceded the commoditizing open-model layer (Chinese models ~61% of top-model OpenRouter token share, Meta's Llama below 1%) and retreated into the compute layer it can fund and chip and China, stuck at 7nm behind an export-control wall (~5x behind at the leading edge, projected to ~17x by 2027; ~$650bn US hyperscaler 2026 capex), largely cannot - bullish for US compute. The trade the thesis implies (research, not advice): long the IG balance sheet + captive demand + power/land + US leading-edge compute; wary of the levered single-customer GPU-owning neocloud and the open-weight model business. An appendix scores the full US neocloud field (18 players) on debt raised, cost of debt, and a self-computed CAPM cost of equity: the debt gap narrows because the neoclouds borrow against their hyperscaler customers' credit (IREN's 6.00% investment-grade GPU facility on the Microsoft contract; Hut 8 / TeraWulf / Cipher investment-grade project bonds at ~6.1% on Google springing-guarantees; CoreWeave's 5.9% IG tranche on pledged customer contracts), but the cost-of-EQUITY gap does not close (hyperscalers ~8-11% vs neoclouds ~15-23%) - which is why 'the anchor becomes a competitor' is the real risk. Builds on study 27 (the AI capital cycle) and study 35 (AI capex by company).
